name: Namazu
description: Namazu is a giant catfish in Japanese mythology said to cause earthquakes. It dwells in the mud beneath the Japanese islands, primarily in the area of Kaname-ishi (keystone) at Kashima Shrine, where the deity Takemikazuchi holds it down with a large stone. When Takemikazuchi relaxes or loses his grip, Namazu thrashes about, causing the earth to shake. Different depictions and myths offer varying explanations for its actions, sometimes portraying it as a mischievous or vengeful creature, while others suggest it is simply acting according to its nature. After major earthquakes, ukiyo-e prints of the Namazu, known as 'Namazu-e', became popular, often depicting the catfish being subdued or rewarding humans with gold for their suffering. These prints served as both news reports and a form of social commentary, reflecting the anxieties and hopes of the populace in the face of natural disasters. The Namazu represents the unpredictable and powerful forces of nature, a constant reminder of the vulnerability of human existence in the face of seismic activity.
subtitle: The Earthquake Catfish of Japan
appearance: Namazu is depicted as a colossal catfish, often much larger than any known real-world catfish. Its scales are usually smooth and dark, and it possesses long, prominent barbels. Its body is muscular and powerful, capable of generating immense force. The size of the Namazu varies across different depictions, ranging from several meters to being large enough to span entire islands.
habitat: Namazu dwells beneath the Japanese islands, in the deep mud and waterways that lie beneath the earth. Its primary location is often associated with the Kaname-ishi (keystone) at Kashima Shrine.
behavior: Namazu is typically portrayed as restless and prone to movement. When it thrashes and moves beneath the earth, it causes earthquakes. Some myths depict it as a mischievous or vengeful creature deliberately causing chaos, while others suggest it is simply acting on instinct or due to the weakening of the deity holding it in place. After earthquakes, Namazu-e often depict it interacting with humans, sometimes being punished and sometimes rewarding them.

keyMyths.title: The Keystone and the Catfish
keyMyths.description: Takemikazuchi, a powerful deity, uses the Kaname-ishi (keystone) at Kashima Shrine to hold down the Namazu. When the deity's vigilance wanes, or the keystone weakens, the Namazu thrashes, causing earthquakes.

keyMyths.title: Namazu-e and Earthquake Relief
keyMyths.description: After major earthquakes, 'Namazu-e' prints became popular, depicting the Namazu in various scenarios, often being subdued or rewarding humans. These prints served as news, social commentary, and even a form of earthquake insurance.

sources.author: Carmen Blacker
sources.work: The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan
sources.description: Discusses the role of Namazu in Japanese folklore and its connection to earthquakes.
sources.text: Discusses the role of Namazu in Japanese folklore and its connection to earthquakes.

sources.author: Gregory Smits
sources.work: Seismic Japan: Earthquakes and Culture in the Edo Period
sources.description: Examines the cultural impact of earthquakes in Japan, including the Namazu myth and Namazu-e prints.
sources.text: Examines the cultural impact of earthquakes in Japan, including the Namazu myth and Namazu-e prints.

worshipPractices.name: Earthquake Prevention Charms
worshipPractices.description: Charms and amulets depicting Namazu being subdued by a deity, often Takemikazuchi, are used for protection against earthquakes. These charms are believed to provide safety and stability during seismic events. They are purchased and displayed in homes and businesses to ward off misfortune.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings at Kashima Shrine
worshipPractices.description: Although not directly worshipped, offerings are sometimes made at Kashima Shrine, associated with Takemikazuchi, the deity who controls Namazu. These offerings seek to strengthen Takemikazuchi's power and, by extension, restrain the earthquake-causing catfish. The offerings are aimed at ensuring stability and peace.

festivals.name: Earthquake Memorial Days
festivals.timing: Varying dates commemorating specific earthquake disasters
festivals.description: While not directly related to Namazu worship, memorial days for significant earthquakes serve as a reminder of the creature's power. Rituals and prayers are offered for the repose of those who perished and for the prevention of future disasters, acknowledging the potential for devastation.

cultural.modernLegacy: Namazu remains a potent symbol of earthquakes in Japan, appearing in educational materials, disaster preparedness campaigns, and popular culture. The image of Namazu is used to raise awareness about earthquake safety and preparedness, serving as a reminder of the country's vulnerability to seismic activity. Additionally, Namazu motifs are incorporated into art and design, reflecting the enduring impact of this mythical creature on the Japanese psyche.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Jörmungandr is a giant serpent encircling Midgard (Earth), and its movements are believed to cause earthquakes and sea disturbances, similar to Namazu's role in Japanese folklore.

Typhon, a monstrous serpentine giant, was imprisoned beneath Mount Etna. His struggles to escape were said to cause volcanic eruptions and earthquakes, echoing Namazu's subterranean origin and earthquake-inducing behavior.
Shesha is a giant serpent upon whom Vishnu rests, and is often depicted as supporting the Earth. When Shesha yawns, the earth shakes, similar to Namazu causing earthquakes in Japanese mythology.

Rūaumoko is the Māori god of earthquakes and volcanoes. He is believed to still be in his mother's womb (Papa, the Earth Mother) and his movements cause earthquakes, like Namazu writhing beneath the islands.

associations.name: Catfish
associations.significance: Namazu is literally a giant catfish, embodying the creature's form and characteristics.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: Namazu's movements beneath the earth are directly responsible for earthquakes.

associations.name: Chaos
associations.significance: Namazu's unpredictable nature and destructive power symbolize chaos and disruption.

associations.name: Subterranean
associations.significance: Namazu dwells beneath the surface, representing the hidden and unknown aspects of the earth.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Namazu is generally depicted as a singular entity, a unique source of seismic activity.

associations.name: Mud Brown
associations.significance: This represents the muddy depths where Namazu resides and from where the tremors originate.

associations.name: Unpredictability
associations.significance: Namazu's actions are sudden and without warning, embodying the unpredictable nature of earthquakes.

companions.name: Takemikazuchi
companions.description: Takemikazuchi is a powerful thunder deity who keeps Namazu subdued with the Kaname-ishi (keystone) at Kashima Shrine. He is constantly vigilant, trying to prevent Namazu from causing earthquakes.

companions.name: Futsunushi
companions.description: Futsunushi is a sword deity often associated with Takemikazuchi, and together they work to control and suppress Namazu. He provides aid and support in restraining the great catfish.

companions.name: Ebisu
companions.description: Ebisu, one of the Seven Lucky Gods, is sometimes depicted with Namazu, and is said to benefit from the earthquakes caused by Namazu as they bring wealth to the surface. He is a complicated figure who has benefited from the earthquakes caused by Namazu.

companions.name: Kaname-ishi
companions.description: The Kaname-ishi is the keystone used by Takemikazuchi to pin Namazu down and prevent earthquakes. It's a sacred stone possessing immense power.

extendedContent.title: Origin and Nature of Namazu
extendedContent.content:
Namazu, the earthquake-causing catfish, is a relatively latecomer to Japanese folklore, gaining prominence primarily in the Edo period (1603-1868). Prior to this, earthquakes were often attributed to angry dragons or other supernatural forces. The specific origins of Namazu are somewhat obscure, but its connection to water and subterranean realms is crucial. Catfish, being bottom-dwellers, naturally lend themselves to the idea of inhabiting the depths beneath the earth. The association with earthquakes likely stemmed from observations of catfish becoming agitated before seismic events, a phenomenon possibly linked to sensitivity to electrical currents or changes in water pressure.

Unlike deities who often possess complex genealogies, Namazu typically appears without a clear parentage or creation myth. Its existence is simply presented as a given, a powerful force residing beneath the land. This lack of origin story reinforces its role as a natural, almost elemental, being. The concept of Namazu resonated with the anxieties of a populace living in a seismically active region. The simplicity of the explanation – a giant fish thrashing about – provided a tangible, albeit mythical, cause for the unpredictable and devastating nature of earthquakes.

Further cementing Namazu's nature is its association with the deity Takemikazuchi of Kashima Shrine. Takemikazuchi, a powerful god of thunder and swords, is depicted as the one tasked with suppressing Namazu. This divine control highlights the inherent chaos and destructive potential attributed to the catfish. The image of Takemikazuchi pinning down Namazu with the Kaname-ishi (keystone) represents the struggle between order and chaos, a recurring theme in Japanese mythology.

extendedContent.title: Encounters in Myth and Art
extendedContent.content:
While Namazu doesn't feature in classical Japanese myths like the Kojiki or Nihon Shoki, its presence is strongly felt in Edo-period art and popular culture, particularly after major earthquakes. The Ansei Edo earthquake of 1855 triggered an explosion of *namazu-e* (catfish prints), cheap woodblock prints depicting various scenes related to Namazu. These prints offer invaluable insights into the popular understanding and anxieties surrounding earthquakes at the time.

In these prints, Namazu isn't always portrayed as a purely malevolent force. Some depict it as a redistributor of wealth, shaking up the social order and benefiting those who were previously disadvantaged. Other prints showcase Namazu being subdued by various deities or even ordinary people, suggesting a degree of agency and resilience in the face of natural disasters. The specific imagery varies greatly, reflecting the diverse perspectives and anxieties of the artists and their audiences.

One common depiction shows Namazu being confronted by Takemikazuchi, often accompanied by other deities or figures. The struggle between the god and the catfish is a recurring motif, emphasizing the constant battle against the forces of nature. In other instances, Namazu is shown interacting with human society, sometimes causing chaos and other times inadvertently bringing about positive change. These varied portrayals showcase the complex and evolving nature of Namazu's role in Japanese folklore.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Meaning of Namazu
extendedContent.content:
Namazu's symbolism extends beyond a simple explanation for earthquakes. It represents the unpredictable and uncontrollable forces of nature, serving as a constant reminder of humanity's vulnerability. The association with the subterranean world further links it to the hidden, unknown aspects of existence. Earthquakes, like other natural disasters, can be seen as a disruption of the established order, a sudden and violent intrusion of the unknown into the familiar.

The Edo-period *namazu-e* reveal a deeper layer of symbolism. As mentioned earlier, some prints portray Namazu as a force of social upheaval, redistributing wealth and challenging the existing power structures. This interpretation suggests that earthquakes, while destructive, can also act as a catalyst for change, dismantling old systems and creating opportunities for new beginnings. In this context, Namazu becomes a symbol of both destruction and renewal.

Furthermore, Namazu's association with Takemikazuchi, the god of thunder and swords, highlights the tension between control and chaos. The image of Takemikazuchi pinning down Namazu represents the human desire to control and understand the natural world. However, the fact that Namazu occasionally escapes this control serves as a reminder of the limitations of human power. The perpetual struggle between the god and the catfish underscores the ongoing negotiation between humanity and the forces of nature.

extendedContent.title: Cultural Significance and Enduring Legacy
extendedContent.content:
Namazu's cultural significance lies in its ability to capture the collective anxieties and coping mechanisms of a society living in a seismically active region. The *namazu-e* prints, produced in the aftermath of devastating earthquakes, served not only as a form of entertainment but also as a way to process trauma and find meaning in the face of destruction. These prints provided a visual language for understanding and grappling with the unpredictable forces of nature.

The enduring legacy of Namazu can be seen in its continued presence in Japanese popular culture. It appears in various forms of media, from anime and manga to video games and literature. While the specific interpretations may vary, the core concept of Namazu as an earthquake-causing entity remains consistent. This continued relevance underscores the enduring power of mythology to shape our understanding of the world around us.

Beyond entertainment, Namazu also serves as a reminder of the importance of disaster preparedness and community resilience. By embodying the unpredictable nature of earthquakes, Namazu encourages a proactive approach to mitigating the risks associated with seismic activity. The stories and images associated with Namazu serve as a cultural touchstone, reminding people of the importance of working together to overcome adversity and rebuild after disasters. The creature’s image is still used today in disaster prevention campaigns, solidifying its place in the collective consciousness.

shortDescription: Namazu is a giant catfish in Japanese mythology that causes earthquakes. It lives under the islands of Japan and is subdued by the deity Takemikazuchi.
